english: seven mountains surrounding the Sumeru.

also mú-stegs-čan (mu stegs can) Ssk. तीर्थिका (overlooking the word stegs (stegs)) it is gen. explained in an intellectual sense, so by Cunningham: adherents of the doctrine of finite existence (Bhilsa Topes), Cs.: the doctrine of perpetual duration or of perpetual annihilation(?); but should not rather mú-stegs (mu stegs) be the same as ˳báb-stegs (bab stegs) (v. ˳báb-pa (bab pa)), being a literal translation of तीर्थ. and therefore prop. a Brahmanic ascetic (v. Ssk. dict.), in Buddhist literature always equivalent to Brahmanist, Non-Buddhist, heretic (infidel)? — 3. Sch has besides: mú-la (mu la) in a circle, continuously; mu-ltar (mu ltar) or mú-nas (mu nas) = bźin-du (bzhin du) C.; in W they say: *mu čig-la bor* (*mu cig la bor*) throw it together on a heap!

1. W. desire, appetite, *zan za-če* (*zan za ce*) or *čaṅ túṅ-čëi mú-ge rag* (*chang thung ce'i mu ge rag*) I have a longing for food, for beer; mú-ge-čan (mu ge can) fond of dainties, lickerish, of men and animals. — 2. B. and col., famine, mú-ge (mu ge) ˳byuṅ ('byung) Dzl., Mil. a famine is caused, breaks out.
nonsense, smrá-ba (smra ba) Stg. to talk nonsense.

pearl frq., mú-tig-rgyan (mu tig rgyan) a pearl ornament Cs; mu-tig-čuṅ-po (mu tig chung po), mu-tig-drá-ba (mu tig dra ba) Glr. garland formed of pearls; mu-tig-p'reṅ (mu tig phreng) string of pearls.
mother of pearl Sch.(?).
Ssk. saint, ascetic, anchorite, chiefly in names: Sā-kya-mu-ni (Sā kya mu ni) the saint of the Sākyas, Buddha.
Sch. = mu-tig (mu tig)(?).
Glr., Mil. a precious stone, of a dark blue, yet inferior to the azure-stone, occasionally used for rosaries; mention is also made of mu-mén dmár-po (mu men dmar po) Wdn.
(?) colt's foot, Tussilago farfara Lh.
brimstone, sulphur Med., mú-zi-čan (mu zi can) containing sulphur, sulphurous; mú-zii skyúr-rtsi (mu zi'i skyur rtsi) (snum (snum) Schr.) sulphuric acid Cs.(?).
hoop, of casks etc. Sch.
Ssk., root; particular roots, such as those of Arum campanulatum, so perh. Lt.
sometimes for mú-ge (mu ge).

1. sbst. moth, worm, múg-ma (mug ma) id. Glr., also múṅ-ma (mung ma); gós-mug (gos mug) clothes-moth, bál-mug (bal mug) id., lcágs-mug (lcags mug) a worm that eats iron away (?) Cs.; múg-zan (mug zan) moth-eaten, destroyed by worms Cs. — 2. vb. with yid- (yid-), yi- (yi-), resp. t'ugs- (thugs-), to despair Pth.; blo múg-po (blo mug po) a gloomy, doleful way of thinking Sch.

1. sbst. obscurity, darkness, frq. — mún-pai smàg-rúm (mun pa'i smag rum) id., frq.; mún-pa-nas mún-par ˳gro (mun pa nas mun par 'gro) Dzl. they wander in eternal darkness; mún-pa sél-ba (mun pa sel ba) to lighten the darkness; frq. fig. with and without bloi (blo'i). — 2. adj. obscure, dark. — 3. vb. in W., mun soṅ (mun song) he has become insensible. — Comp. mún-k'aṅ (mun khang) dark room, e.g. the sanctuary containing the images of the gods Glr.; prison Cs. — mún-k'uṅ (mun khung) Dzl. prison, dungeon. — *mun-t'ig* (*mun thig*) Lh., mun-k'ród (mun khrod) Dzl., *mun-nág* (*mun nag*) W., C., mun-brág (mun brag) Sch. and Lh.(?) close darkness. — *mun-d'úl* (*mun dul*), or *mun-nyúg táṅ-če* (*mun nyug tang ce*) W. to grope in the dark. — *mún-ču* (*mun chu*), *núm-ču* (*num chu*) W. the dusk of evening, *mún(-ču) rub* (*mun (-chu) rub*) sets in. — *mún-(s)pe-ra táṅ-če* (*mun -(s)pe ra tang ce*) W. to talk confusedly, wildly. — mun-sprúl (mun sprul) Tar. 56,17, to judge by the context: ignorance, stupidity; so Schf. — mun-sríbs (mun sribs) Lex. the darkness of night. — mun-sró (mun sro) furious passion, *mún-sro yoṅ dug* (*mun sro yong dug*) W. he rages in his passion. — *mun-srós* (*mun sros*) = *mún-ču* (*mun chu*) W.
(Hind.) W. wax.

1. termin. of mu (mu), hence mur-t'ug (mur thug) to the extremity, till the end of Cs.; perh. also mur-dúm (mur dum) (or -zlum (-zlum)?) Ld. dull, of knives, hatchets; múr-˳dug (mur 'dug) = mú-stegs-pa (mu stegs pa) Sch. — 2. gills of fish.
the temples Sch.; mur-˳grám (mur 'gram) id. Cs.; jaw, jaw-bone Sch. — mur-t'ór (mur thor) ulcers in the mouth Sch.
1. to gnaw, to destroy by gnawing, to bite asunder, e.g. bones Thgr. — 2. to masticate, to chew(?).
